description: Loose French-hen-inspired folk cookbook recipe art. The look should feel hand-painted, coarse, confident, and materially wet, not photorealistic or digitally polished.
medium: Loose watercolor and gouache on bright white cold-press paper, with obvious wet pigment blooms, dappled stippling, broken dry-brush texture, uneven gouache opacity, and selective dark ink/paint contours. Preserve visible brush marks and pigment pooling; do not smooth them away.
palette: Warm ochres, siennas, mustards, olives, browned-food tones, cream paper, faint sky-blue washes, jade/teal paint accents, dark brown or black ink, and small warm red highlights.
composition: Consistent three-quarter recipe-card food perspective with the action or finished food centered and scaled for card use: clear silhouette, even breathing room, but no broad dead bands of blank paper above or beside the subject. Use the same cookware angle when an object repeats. Step art should fill the usable canvas enough to read at mobile width; optional loose teal accent marks only when they help a hero composition, never inside step art.
lighting: Soft natural kitchen light suggested through watercolor wash and irregular shadow, not photorealistic studio lighting or glossy metal rendering. Food should be readable and appetizing while retaining handmade looseness.
rendering: Naive-but-assured folk-art cookbook plate feel: large economical strokes, coarse stippled texture, visible wet-on-wet pooling, uneven edges, dry-brush skips, confident black/brown calligraphic contours, and simplified forms. No smooth gradients, no airbrushed finish, no hyper-detailed realism.
constraints: Asset is art only; the HTML supplies all typography, layout, tables, timing, and labels.
Show the specific food state for this step clearly enough that a cook can recognize it, but render it with loose brushwork rather than photographic detail.
Keep cookware and food fully visible while using a tight, balanced crop that can be trimmed and placed full-bleed in a card.
Avoid placing the subject low, tiny, or off to one side with large removable paper margins.
Do not draw words, numbers, measurement labels, arrows, callouts, badges, watermarks, or interface chrome.
Use the reference image for material handling and brush language only; do not copy the rooster/chick subject, signature, or border.
negative: No text, captions, ingredient labels, fake handwriting, UI cards, arrows, diagram lines, watermark, photorealistic product ad, glossy digital painting, smooth gradients, stock-photo realism, slick editorial food photo, shiny 3D render, airbrushed finish, tiny over-detailed garnish, plastic-looking food, extra hands, distorted cookware, cluttered background.

oneShotExamples.description: Use as a style reference for loose watercolor/gouache brushwork, pigment pooling, stippling, warm folk-art palette, teal accent marks, and hand-painted cookbook-plate feeling. Do not copy the rooster subject, chick, signature, border, or page layout.

htmlTheme.description: Neutral page defaults for recipe cards. Host sites should override these tokens in their own renderer CSS.
